d: Design systems use a three-layer token model: (1) Primitive tokens store raw values like hex colors (#1A1A1A), pixel sizes (16), font weights (600). Named by value: gray-900, blue-500, spacing-4. (2) Semantic tokens are intent-based and reference primitives via aliases. Named by purpose: text-primary, surface-danger, action-primary-default, border-subtle. (3) Component tokens are application-specific and reference semantics. Named by usage: button-bg-primary, card-border-radius, input-padding-x. Benefits: single point of change, theme support, platform consistency, designer-developer alignment.

Primitive tokens hold raw design values with no semantic meaning. Types: hex colors (#FFFFFF, #1A1A1A, #2563EB), pixel sizes (4, 8, 12, 16, 24, 32, 48, 64), font weights (400, 500, 600, 700), opacity values (0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0). Naming convention: name by value not purpose. Examples: gray-100, gray-900, blue-500, red-600, spacing-4, spacing-8, radius-4, radius-8. Primitives form the foundation that semantic tokens reference.

Semantic tokens describe intent and reference primitive tokens via aliases. They enable theming: changing the primitive reference updates all semantics automatically. Examples: text-primary aliases gray-900 (light) or gray-100 (dark), surface-default aliases white (light) or gray-900 (dark), action-primary-default aliases blue-600, border-subtle aliases gray-200 (light) or gray-700 (dark), surface-danger aliases red-50, text-on-action aliases white. RULE: All design elements must use semantic tokens, never primitives directly.

Component tokens are scoped to specific UI components and reference semantic tokens. Examples: button-bg-primary references action-primary-default, button-text-primary references text-on-action, card-border-radius references radius-lg, input-padding-x references spacing-sm, input-border-default references border-default, modal-overlay-bg references surface-overlay. Component tokens provide the most granular control. Changing a component token only affects that component, while changing a semantic token affects all components referencing it.

RULE: Never use primitive tokens directly in designs. Always go through the semantic layer. RULE: Semantic tokens enable theming. Change the primitive reference and all semantics update. RULE: Component tokens reference semantics, not primitives. The chain is always: Component -> Semantic -> Primitive. RULE: If you need a new value, add a primitive first, then create the semantic reference, then the component reference if needed. Breaking the chain makes theming impossible and creates maintenance debt.

Figma supports four variable types: (1) Color: hex values with opacity support. Use for all color decisions including fills, strokes, text color. (2) Number: pixel values, percentages. Use for spacing, sizing, border-radius, opacity values, font-size, line-height. (3) String: text values. Use for font families, content strings, labels. (4) Boolean: true/false. Use for visibility toggles, feature flags, showing/hiding layers. Additionally, any variable can be an Alias that references another variable. Aliases are the KEY mechanism for token layering where semantic tokens reference primitive tokens.

Variable aliases allow one variable to reference another variable's value. This is the foundation of the three-layer token model. To create an alias: click the variable value field, then click the alias icon (chain link), then select the target variable. Example: semantic variable text-primary aliases primitive variable gray-900. When gray-900 changes, text-primary automatically updates. Aliases can chain: component token -> semantic token -> primitive token. Aliases work across collections. A semantic collection can alias variables from a primitives collection.

A Variable Collection is a set of variables that share the same modes. Organize collections by domain: Colors, Spacing, Typography, Elevation, Border. Alternatively organize by token layer: Primitives, Semantics, Components. Each collection can have multiple modes (e.g., Light/Dark for a Colors collection). All variables within a single collection share the same set of modes. You cannot have a variable with modes that differ from other variables in its collection. Best practice: keep collections focused. A Primitives/Colors collection and a Primitives/Spacing collection is cleaner than one massive Primitives collection.

A Mode is a set of alternate values for all variables in a collection. Common mode configurations: Light/Dark (color theming), Compact/Comfortable (density), Mobile/Desktop (responsive sizing), Brand-A/Brand-B (multi-brand). The default mode is the first mode in the collection and is applied when no mode is explicitly set. To apply a mode: select any frame, open the layer section in the right panel, choose the mode. All children of that frame inherit the mode. Mode limits by plan: Free=4 modes per collection, Professional=10, Organization/Enterprise=20.

Modes are applied at the frame level and inherited by all children. To apply: select a frame, in the right panel under the layer section, choose a mode for a given collection. Nested frames can override the parent mode. Example: top-level frame set to Light mode, a nested card frame set to Dark mode creates an inverted card. RULE: Design with the default mode first, then verify in all other modes. RULE: Use top-level frames for page-level mode switching. Use nested frames for component-level inversions (e.g., dark footer on light page).

Step-by-step dark mode setup: (1) Create Primitives collection with all raw color values. One mode is sufficient since primitives are absolute values. (2) Create Semantic collection with two modes: Light (default) and Dark. (3) In Light mode, alias semantic vars to light primitives: text-primary -> gray-900, surface-default -> white, border-default -> gray-200. (4) In Dark mode, alias semantic vars to dark primitives: text-primary -> gray-100, surface-default -> gray-900, border-default -> gray-700. (5) Apply semantic variables to all design elements. (6) Switch mode on top-level frame to preview dark mode.

Dark mode design rules: Background and surface colors invert (white becomes gray-900, gray-50 becomes gray-800). Text colors invert (gray-900 becomes gray-100). Brand colors may need adjustment for contrast on dark backgrounds. Shadows should reduce in dark mode since dark surfaces don't cast visible shadows. Borders become more visible in dark mode to define edges where shadow no longer works. Always check contrast ratios in both modes: 4.5:1 minimum for AA normal text, 3:1 for large text. Elevation in dark mode is conveyed by lighter surface colors rather than shadows.

Multi-brand systems share the same semantic and component layers but swap primitives per brand. Approach 1: Create separate Primitives collections per brand and swap which collection is active. Approach 2: Use extended modes on the Primitives collection (Brand-A mode, Brand-B mode). The semantic layer remains identical. Only primitive references change. Example: semantic token brand-primary in Brand-A mode aliases blue-600, in Brand-B mode aliases green-600. All components automatically adapt when the brand mode is switched on the frame. This enables white-label products from a single design system.

t: When to Use Styles vs Variables
d: USE VARIABLES FOR: colors (fills, strokes, text), spacing (padding, gap, margin), sizing (width, height), border-radius, opacity, font-size, line-height, any value that needs aliasing or multi-mode support. USE STYLES FOR: typography composites (font family + weight + size + line-height + letter-spacing combined), complex effects (multi-layer shadows), gradients (linear, radial, angular, diamond), grid layouts. HYBRID APPROACH: Variables define individual values, Styles compose them. Example: a text style references a variable for font-size. Variables generate cleaner code tokens in dev mode. Styles support composite values that variables cannot yet represent.

Color styles define reusable color values: solid colors, linear gradients, radial gradients, angular gradients, diamond gradients. Naming: use slash hierarchy for grouping. Examples: Colors/Primary/500, Colors/Neutral/100, Colors/Success/Default, Colors/Gradients/Brand. Apply color styles to: fills, strokes, text color. RULE: Prefer variables over color styles when theming support (multi-mode) is needed. Color styles remain useful for gradients since variables do not support gradient values. When migrating from styles to variables, create a variable for each solid color style, then update references.

Text styles bundle multiple typography properties: font-family, font-weight, font-size, line-height, letter-spacing, paragraph-spacing, text-decoration, text-transform. Naming convention: Typography/Heading/H1, Typography/Body/Regular, Typography/Caption/Small. RULE: Always define text styles. Never use raw font values in designs. RULE: Limit to 8-12 text styles maximum for consistency. Typical type scale: Display(48-72px), H1(36-40px), H2(28-32px), H3(24px), H4(20px), Body-L(18px), Body(16px), Body-S(14px), Caption(12px), Overline(10-11px).

Line-height ratios by context: headings use 1.1-1.3 (tight, for display and headers), body text uses 1.4-1.6 (comfortable reading), tight body uses 1.2 (UI labels, buttons). Specific values: Display line-height 1.1 (52.8-79.2px for 48-72px font), H1 line-height 1.2 (43.2-48px), Body line-height 1.5 (24px for 16px font), Caption line-height 1.4 (16.8px for 12px font). Letter-spacing: headings may use negative tracking (-0.02em to -0.01em), body uses default (0), all-caps/overline use positive tracking (0.05em-0.1em). Paragraph spacing: typically 0.5x to 1x the font-size.

Drop shadow examples: Subtle/Level-1: x:0 y:1 blur:2 spread:0 color:rgba(0,0,0,0.05). Small/Level-2: x:0 y:2 blur:4 spread:0 color:rgba(0,0,0,0.10). Medium/Level-3: x:0 y:4 blur:12 spread:0 color:rgba(0,0,0,0.12). Large/Level-4: x:0 y:8 blur:24 spread:0 color:rgba(0,0,0,0.15). XLarge/Level-5: x:0 y:16 blur:48 spread:0 color:rgba(0,0,0,0.20). Inner shadow: for inset effects and pressed states. Layer blur: for frosted glass / glassmorphism effects (blur:8-24px on a semi-transparent surface). Naming: Effects/Shadow/Small, Effects/Shadow/Medium, Effects/Shadow/Large, Effects/Blur/Background.

Elevation creates visual hierarchy through layered depth. Level-0: flat, no shadow (default surface). Level-1: subtle lift (cards, tiles). y:1-2, blur:2-4. Level-2: raised (dropdowns, popovers). y:4, blur:8-12. Level-3: floating (sticky headers, FABs). y:8, blur:16-24. Level-4: overlay (modals, dialogs). y:16, blur:32-48. Level-5: highest (toasts, notifications). y:24, blur:48+. Each level increases y-offset, blur radius, and shadow opacity. In dark mode, replace shadows with lighter surface colors to convey elevation since shadows are invisible on dark backgrounds.

Grid style definitions per breakpoint: Desktop (1280px+): 12 columns, auto width, 24px gutter, 64px margin. Tablet (768-1279px): 8 columns, auto width, 16-24px gutter, 32px margin. Mobile (320-767px): 4 columns, auto width, 16px gutter, 16px margin. Row grids for baseline alignment: 4px or 8px baseline grid ensures vertical rhythm. Uniform grids: equal spacing in both directions for icon grids or tile layouts. Naming: Grids/Desktop-12col, Grids/Tablet-8col, Grids/Mobile-4col. RULE: Apply grid styles to frames, not individual elements. Max content width typically 1200-1440px.

Variable scoping restricts where a variable appears in suggestion dropdowns. Scope options: fill color, stroke color, text color, width, height, gap, padding (all/top/right/bottom/left), corner-radius, font-size, font-weight, line-height, letter-spacing, opacity. Why scope: prevents misuse. A color variable scoped to 'fill color' will not appear when setting spacing. A spacing variable scoped to 'gap' and 'padding' will not pollute color suggestions. To set scope: select variable, click scope icon in variables panel, check applicable properties. RULE: Always scope variables to prevent polluted suggestions and enforce correct usage.

Scoping patterns: Color primitives: scope to fill, stroke, text color. Spacing variables: scope to gap, padding (all directions), width, height. Border-radius variables: scope to corner-radius only. Font-size variables: scope to font-size only. Opacity variables: scope to opacity only. Icon-size variables: scope to width, height. Semantic color tokens inherit scope from the context: surface colors scope to fill, text colors scope to text color, border colors scope to stroke. RULE: Scope by collection as well. Typography spacing vars should not appear for color fields. The fewer irrelevant suggestions designers see, the faster they work.

Token naming format: category/property/variant/state. Use slash or hyphen separation consistently. Color tokens: color/primary/500, color/surface/default, color/text/primary, color/text/secondary, color/border/default, color/border/subtle, color/action/primary/default, color/action/primary/hover. Spacing tokens: spacing/xs(4px), spacing/sm(8px), spacing/md(16px), spacing/lg(24px), spacing/xl(32px), spacing/2xl(48px), spacing/3xl(64px). Border-radius tokens: radius/none(0), radius/sm(4px), radius/md(8px), radius/lg(12px), radius/xl(16px), radius/full(9999px). Elevation: elevation/1, elevation/2, elevation/3, elevation/4, elevation/5.

RULE: Be consistent. Pick one naming convention (slash-separated or hyphen-separated) and enforce it across all tokens. RULE: Names should describe purpose, not appearance. Use 'primary' not 'blue', 'danger' not 'red', 'text-primary' not 'dark-gray'. RULE: Use size scales consistently: xs, sm, md, lg, xl, 2xl, 3xl OR 100-900 for color shades. Anti-patterns: mixing camelCase and kebab-case, using color names as semantic tokens (btn-blue), inconsistent hierarchy depth (some tokens 2 levels, others 5 levels), abbreviations that are ambiguous (bg vs background — pick one and be consistent).

Publishing workflow: (1) Click the blue 'Publish' button in the assets panel (or File > Publish styles and variables). (2) Published assets include: components, styles, and variables. (3) Consumers enable the library: Assets panel > Team Libraries icon > toggle on the desired library. (4) When publisher pushes an update, consumers see a notification banner to accept or dismiss updates. (5) Consumers review changes before accepting. RULE: Always add a description when publishing updates so consumers understand what changed. RULE: Use branching for major library changes and merge only after verification.

Recommended library file structure: (1) Foundation library: contains all variables (primitives, semantics), color styles, text styles, effect styles, grid styles. No components. (2) Core component library: buttons, inputs, checkboxes, selects, toggles, badges, tags, avatars, tooltips. Depends on Foundation library. (3) Pattern library: cards, forms, navigation, headers, footers, modals, tables. Depends on Core library. Benefits: smaller publish scope, faster updates, clear dependency chain. RULE: Foundation changes are rare and high-impact. Core changes are moderate. Pattern changes are frequent and low-risk.

Governance essentials: Assign a design system owner or team responsible for maintenance. Document usage guidelines for each component and token. Version components with clear changelog. Deprecation policy: mark component as deprecated with a description explaining why, provide a migration path to the replacement, remove deprecated components after 2 release cycles. Contribution model: propose (file issue/request) > review (design system team evaluates) > approve (meets standards) > publish (team publishes update). Regular audits: check for detached instances, unused components, hardcoded values, inconsistent token usage.

Audit checklist: (1) Detached instances: components that were detached from the library and are now local. Reattach or update the source. (2) Hardcoded values: colors, spacing, or fonts applied directly instead of using variables/styles. Replace with tokens. (3) Missing variables: design properties using literal values that should be tokenized. (4) Unused components: published components with zero instances. Consider deprecation. (5) Inconsistent naming: tokens or components that break naming conventions. (6) Accessibility: check all color combinations for WCAG contrast compliance. (7) Orphaned styles: styles that are defined but not used anywhere. Schedule audits quarterly.

Standard 4px-base spacing scale: spacing/0(0px), spacing/0.5(2px), spacing/1(4px), spacing/1.5(6px), spacing/2(8px), spacing/3(12px), spacing/4(16px), spacing/5(20px), spacing/6(24px), spacing/8(32px), spacing/10(40px), spacing/12(48px), spacing/16(64px), spacing/20(80px), spacing/24(96px). Simplified named scale: xs=4px, sm=8px, md=16px, lg=24px, xl=32px, 2xl=48px, 3xl=64px. Use spacing variables for: padding, gap, margin (via auto layout), component internal spacing. RULE: All spacing must come from the scale. No arbitrary values like 13px or 27px. The 4px grid ensures visual consistency.

Where to apply spacing tokens: (1) Padding: internal space within components. Button padding: spacing/2(8px) vertical, spacing/4(16px) horizontal. Card padding: spacing/4(16px) or spacing/6(24px). (2) Gap: space between sibling elements in auto layout. Form field gap: spacing/4(16px). Button group gap: spacing/2(8px). Card list gap: spacing/4(16px) or spacing/6(24px). (3) Section spacing: spacing/12(48px) to spacing/16(64px) between major page sections. (4) Inline spacing: spacing/1(4px) to spacing/2(8px) between icon and label. RULE: Apply spacing via auto layout properties, not manual positioning. This ensures consistent and responsive layouts.

Standard color palette structure: Each hue has 10 shades from 50 (lightest) to 950 (darkest). Example gray scale: gray-50(#F9FAFB), gray-100(#F3F4F6), gray-200(#E5E7EB), gray-300(#D1D5DB), gray-400(#9CA3AF), gray-500(#6B7280), gray-600(#4B5563), gray-700(#374151), gray-800(#1F2937), gray-900(#111827), gray-950(#030712). Repeat for primary (blue), success (green), warning (amber/yellow), danger/error (red), info (cyan/blue). Additionally: white(#FFFFFF), black(#000000), and a transparent value. These are all primitive tokens that semantic tokens will reference.

Semantic color token mapping for Light and Dark modes. Surfaces: surface-default (Light:#FFFFFF, Dark:#111827), surface-subtle (Light:#F9FAFB, Dark:#1F2937), surface-muted (Light:#F3F4F6, Dark:#374151). Text: text-primary (Light:#111827, Dark:#F9FAFB), text-secondary (Light:#4B5563, Dark:#9CA3AF), text-disabled (Light:#9CA3AF, Dark:#4B5563). Borders: border-default (Light:#E5E7EB, Dark:#374151), border-subtle (Light:#F3F4F6, Dark:#1F2937). Actions: action-primary-default (Light/Dark:#2563EB), action-primary-hover (Light/Dark:#1D4ED8). Status: success (#16A34A), warning (#D97706), danger (#DC2626), info (#0891B2).

Component token definitions referencing semantic tokens. Button: button-bg-primary -> action-primary-default, button-bg-primary-hover -> action-primary-hover, button-text-primary -> text-on-action(#FFFFFF), button-padding-x -> spacing/4(16px), button-padding-y -> spacing/2(8px), button-radius -> radius/md(8px), button-border -> border-default. Input: input-bg -> surface-default, input-border -> border-default, input-border-focus -> action-primary-default, input-text -> text-primary, input-placeholder -> text-secondary, input-padding-x -> spacing/3(12px), input-padding-y -> spacing/2(8px), input-radius -> radius/md(8px). Card: card-bg -> surface-default, card-border -> border-subtle, card-radius -> radius/lg(12px), card-padding -> spacing/6(24px), card-shadow -> elevation/1.

To implement theme switching in Figma: (1) Ensure all design elements use semantic variables (not primitives, not hardcoded values). (2) Select the outermost frame of your design. (3) In the right panel, under the layer section, find the mode selector for your Semantic Colors collection. (4) Switch from Light to Dark mode. All child elements update instantly. (5) For previewing both themes side-by-side: duplicate the top-level frame, set one to Light and one to Dark. (6) For component-level inversions (e.g., dark hero section on light page), apply Dark mode to just that section's frame. Child frames can override parent modes at any nesting level.

Hybrid approach for typography tokens: Create number variables for font-size and line-height values: type-size/xs(12px), type-size/sm(14px), type-size/md(16px), type-size/lg(18px), type-size/xl(20px), type-size/2xl(24px), type-size/3xl(32px), type-size/4xl(40px), type-size/5xl(48px). Create number variables for line-height: type-leading/tight(1.2), type-leading/normal(1.5), type-leading/relaxed(1.6). Create number variables for letter-spacing: type-tracking/tight(-0.02em), type-tracking/normal(0), type-tracking/wide(0.05em). Then create text styles that reference these variables. This gives you both composability (styles) and tokenization (variables).

To create variables: (1) Open the Variables panel (right sidebar or via main menu). (2) Click '+' to create a new collection or select an existing one. (3) Click 'Create variable' and choose the type: Color, Number, String, or Boolean. (4) Name the variable following your naming convention (e.g., color/primary/500). (5) Set the value for each mode. (6) To create an alias: click the value field, click the alias icon (two overlapping diamonds), search and select the target variable. (7) Set variable scoping: click the variable, open settings, check applicable property scopes. (8) Group variables using slash notation in names (e.g., color/primary/500 creates a 'color > primary' group).

Variables are automatically grouped in the panel using slash (/) notation in names. Example: naming variables color/primary/100, color/primary/500, color/primary/900 creates a nested group: color > primary > 100, 500, 900. This visual grouping helps designers find variables quickly. Groups can be collapsed/expanded in the variables panel. Best practice group structure for primitives: color/gray/50-950, color/blue/50-950, color/red/50-950, spacing/1-24, radius/sm-full. For semantics: color/text/primary, color/text/secondary, color/surface/default, color/border/default, color/action/primary/default.

WCAG 2.1 contrast requirements: AA Normal text (under 24px / 18.66px bold): 4.5:1 minimum contrast ratio. AA Large text (24px+ / 18.66px+ bold): 3:1 minimum contrast ratio. AAA Normal text: 7:1 ratio. AAA Large text: 4.5:1 ratio. UI components and graphical objects: 3:1 against adjacent colors. Check contrast in both light and dark modes. Common failures: light gray text on white (gray-400 on white = 3.0:1, FAILS AA), placeholder text too light, disabled states invisible. Tools: Figma plugins like Stark or A11y - Color Contrast Checker. RULE: Verify all text/background combinations meet AA minimum.

Standard border-radius scale: radius/none: 0px (sharp corners, tables, full-bleed sections). radius/xs: 2px (subtle rounding, tags, small badges). radius/sm: 4px (inputs, buttons in dense UI). radius/md: 8px (cards, buttons, default component radius). radius/lg: 12px (large cards, modals, popovers). radius/xl: 16px (hero sections, large containers). radius/2xl: 24px (prominent cards, marketing sections). radius/full: 9999px (pills, avatars, circular elements). RULE: Nested elements should have smaller radius than their parent. Inner radius = outer radius minus padding. Example: card with radius/lg(12px) and padding 16px, inner element radius = max(0, 12-16) = 0 or use radius/sm(4px).

Icon sizes: icon/xs: 12px (inline with small text), icon/sm: 16px (inline with body text, input icons), icon/md: 20px (default UI icon size), icon/lg: 24px (navigation, prominent icons), icon/xl: 32px (feature icons, empty states), icon/2xl: 48px (illustration icons, onboarding). Touch target minimums: 44x44px minimum for mobile (WCAG), 48x48px recommended (Material), 24x24px minimum for desktop. Button minimum heights: small: 32px, medium: 40px, large: 48px. Input heights: small: 32px, medium: 40px, large: 48px. RULE: Even if the visual icon is 16px, the clickable area must meet touch target minimums using padding.

Standard opacity scale as number variables: opacity/0: 0 (fully transparent), opacity/5: 0.05 (very subtle overlays), opacity/10: 0.10 (subtle hover backgrounds, shadow color), opacity/15: 0.15 (shadow color for elevated elements), opacity/20: 0.20 (disabled state overlays, medium shadows), opacity/40: 0.40 (disabled content, placeholder imagery), opacity/50: 0.50 (half-visible, loading skeletons), opacity/70: 0.70 (backdrop overlays for modals), opacity/80: 0.80 (frosted glass surfaces), opacity/100: 1.0 (fully opaque, default). Common usages: modal backdrop overlay 0.5-0.7 on black, disabled states reduce to 0.4 opacity, hover states apply 0.05-0.1 color overlay.

Version management: Use semantic versioning or date-based versioning for your design system library. When publishing updates, always write a meaningful description: what changed, why, and any migration steps. Categories of changes: (1) Patch: bug fixes, minor visual adjustments (no action needed by consumers). (2) Minor: new components, new variants, new tokens (opt-in, no breaking changes). (3) Major: renamed tokens, restructured components, removed variants (requires consumer migration). Consumers receive update notifications and can review changes before accepting. RULE: Never make breaking changes without advance communication and migration guides.

Density modes control spacing and sizing for different contexts. Create a Spacing collection with modes: Comfortable (default) and Compact. Comfortable values: spacing/sm=8px, spacing/md=16px, spacing/lg=24px, component-padding=16px, component-gap=12px, input-height=40px, button-height=40px. Compact values: spacing/sm=4px, spacing/md=8px, spacing/lg=16px, component-padding=8px, component-gap=8px, input-height=32px, button-height=32px. Apply Compact mode to data-heavy interfaces (tables, dashboards, admin panels). Apply Comfortable to consumer-facing, content-driven pages. Switch at the frame level just like light/dark mode.

Boolean variables control layer visibility and feature flags within components. Examples: show-icon (true/false) toggles icon layer visibility in a button component, show-avatar (true/false) toggles avatar display in a list item, show-description (true/false) toggles description text in a card. To use: create a boolean variable, bind it to a layer's visibility property. When the variable is true, the layer is visible; when false, it is hidden. Boolean variables can also have modes: show-feature-banner could be true in Brand-A mode and false in Brand-B mode. This enables mode-driven feature toggling without creating separate component variants.

Design system maturity checklist: Level 1 (Foundation): Color palette defined as variables with light/dark modes, spacing scale (4px base) as variables, typography scale as text styles (8-12 styles), border-radius scale as variables, shadow/elevation scale as effect styles. Level 2 (Components): All core components built (button, input, select, checkbox, radio, toggle, badge, avatar, tooltip, card, modal, table), components use variables for all properties, components have variants for size/state/type. Level 3 (Governance): Library published and consumed by team, naming conventions documented and enforced, contribution process defined, quarterly audits scheduled, deprecation policy in place. Level 4 (Optimization): Component tokens layer exists, multi-brand support implemented, density modes available, design-to-code token handoff automated.

t: Typography Variables — Setup Workflow (Variables + Styles Together)
d: Typography in Figma requires BOTH text styles AND variables working together. Text styles define the composite (family + weight + size + line-height), variables power the individual values so modes can swap them. RULE: Always create text styles first — Figma does not allow creating typography variables independently from styles. Workflow: (1) Create all text styles with final values. (2) Open Local Variables, create a Typography collection. (3) Create Number variables for font-size (e.g. type/size/xl = 32) and line-height (e.g. type/lineheight/xl = 40). (4) Create String variables for font-family (e.g. type/family/heading = 'Inter') and font-weight (e.g. type/weight/bold = 'Bold' — must match exact weight name in the font). (5) Open each text style, click each property field, apply the matching variable via 'Apply variable'. (6) Set variable scoping: font-size vars → font-size scope only; line-height vars → line-height scope only; font-family vars → font-family scope only. (7) Add modes (Desktop/Mobile) to the Typography collection and set alternate values per mode.

t: Responsive Typography Modes — Desktop and Mobile in a Single Collection
d: Responsive typography is the primary reason to use typography variables over plain text styles. Instead of maintaining separate Desktop and Mobile text style sets, use a single Typography variable collection with two modes: Desktop (default) and Mobile. All font-size and line-height variables carry two values — the Desktop value and the Mobile value. When a frame is set to Mobile mode, every text style that references these variables automatically updates its sizing. Example: type/size/h1 = 48px (Desktop) / 32px (Mobile). type/size/body = 16px (Desktop) / 15px (Mobile). type/lineheight/h1 = 56px (Desktop) / 40px (Mobile). How to apply: select a section frame, in the right panel under variables find the Typography collection, switch to Mobile mode. All type in that frame rescales instantly. RULE: Never maintain two parallel sets of text styles for desktop and mobile — use one set of styles powered by responsive variables. This is the correct modern approach.

meta.rule: One style set + responsive modes > two parallel style sets

t: Typography Variable Types and Naming — String vs Number, T-Shirt Scale
d: Typography variable type selection: FONT SIZE → Number variable. Values are pixel integers (12, 14, 16, 20, 24, 32, 40, 48). FONT SIZE naming use t-shirt scale: type/size/xs, type/size/sm, type/size/md, type/size/lg, type/size/xl, type/size/2xl, type/size/3xl. Avoids fragile numeric names (type/size/32 becomes wrong if the value changes). LINE HEIGHT → Number variable. Mirror the exact same naming as font sizes: type/lineheight/xs, type/lineheight/sm… so each size has a matching line-height. FONT FAMILY → String variable. Values must be the exact font name as it appears in Figma: 'Inter', 'Geist', 'Playfair Display'. Naming: type/family/base, type/family/heading, type/family/mono. FONT WEIGHT → String or Number. Use String if your font uses named weights ('Regular', 'Medium', 'SemiBold', 'Bold') — the string value MUST match exactly what the font calls that weight. Use Number if weights follow numeric naming (400, 500, 600, 700). CRITICAL: mismatched weight names silently fail — the variable applies but the weight does not change. Always verify by typing the weight name exactly as shown in the font picker.

meta.warning: Font weight string must match font's own weight name exactly — mismatches fail silently
